FT_IC1.evidence: Uses NIMH Data Archive ABCD collection release 4.0 (DOI: 10.15154/1,523,041); 21 U.S. recruitment sites; participants aged 9-10 at baseline.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: exploratory factor analysis on test set (n=3,415) and linear mixed-effect regression models examining associations between risk groups and behavioral/neural outcomes at 2-year follow-up.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Journal of Adolescent Health; no indication of duplicate.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: No numeric estimate in any results location where the variable label is phone/SM-specific (e.g., time on phone, time on SM, texting, video chat) paired with an individual-trait outcome. Screen time (weekday and weekend) appears only as one component within the Factor 5 composite. Step 2: The composite risk variable bundles fat/sugar intake, prenatal alcohol/caffeine exposure, parental aggressiveness, ADHD, family violence, family income, parental education, BMI z-score, and screen time into a single score. This composite contains non-phone/non-SM ingredients (nutrition, prenatal exposure, family environment, SES) with no separate phone/SM breakout in the results. NOT_MET under Step 2 composite-bundling rule.

notes: Screen time is Factor 5 in a six-factor composite risk variable. All inferential analyses use this composite risk-group variable (low/medium/high) as the IV; screen time never appears as a standalone IV or DV with its own coefficient against an individual-trait outcome.

FT_IC1.evidence: Analyzes longitudinal ABCD Study data; 21 U.S. sites; baseline 2016-2018; ages 9-11 years; n=3,498.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: Cox proportional hazards regression models examining prospective associations of sleep duration and screen time with BMI transition from overweight/obesity to normal.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in International Journal of Obesity; no indication of duplicate.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: The only screen-time variable analyzed is 'total screen time (hours/day)' — no numeric estimate exists where the variable label is phone/SM-specific (texting, social media, video chat, time on phone, named platform, etc.) paired with an individual-trait outcome. Step 2: The composite 'total screen time' is the ABCD Youth Screen Time Survey aggregate including: viewing/streaming TV shows or movies, watching/streaming videos (e.g., YouTube), playing video games, texting, video chatting (e.g., Skype, FaceTime), and using social media (e.g., Facebook, Instagram, Twitter). This composite includes TV, YouTube streaming, and platform-unspecified video games alongside phone/SM items, with no separate per-modality breakout reported anywhere in this paper. NOT_MET under Step 2 bundling rule.

notes: Screen time is IV predicting BMI transition (HR 0.99, 95% CI 0.96-1.02), but the composite bundles TV, YouTube, video games, texting, video chat, and social media. No per-modality estimates are reported in main tables or any supplementary/sensitivity analyses in this paper.

FT_IC1.evidence: Analyzes ABCD Study data; n=7,445 participants; ages 9-10 at baseline (2016-2018); 21 U.S. sites; prospective longitudinal design.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: weighted multivariable linear regression models examining independent and joint prospective associations of screen time and sleep with BMI and waist circumference at 2-year follow-up.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Childhood Obesity; no indication of duplicate.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 hit: The paper reports separate analyses of screen time subtypes including 'socializing (texting, video chatting, and social media)' vs. BMI and WC (Supplementary Tables S6 and S7). The 'socializing' subtype is composed exclusively of phone/SM/digital-socializing items (texting + video chatting + social media). These modality-specific analyses yield numeric regression coefficients (B) pairing the phone/SM-specific socializing subtype with BMI and waist circumference — both individual physical-health-level outcomes. Methods state: 'We also examined associations of screen time subtypes (TV/movies, video games, socializing) with BMI and WC, adjusting for confounders.' FT-IC2 = MET under Step 1.

FT_IC3.evidence: Regression coefficients (B with 95% CI and p-values) reported for screen time (continuous and categorical) vs. BMI (B=0.32; 95% CI: 0.19, 0.45; p<0.001) and WC (B=0.40; 95% CI: 0.23, 0.56; p<0.001) in main Tables 2 and 3, plus subtype-specific results in Supplementary Tables S6-S7.

FT_IC4.evidence: Quantitative numeric results reported throughout: regression coefficients, 95% CIs, and p-values for all main and supplementary analyses. Multiple inferential tests reported for screen time subtypes, joint combinations with sleep, and sex-stratified models.

FT_EC7_reason: Screen time is the IV (predictor) in all analyses, with BMI and WC as the DVs. EC7 only applies when SM/phone is DV-only; here it is IV-only. EC7 does not apply.
notes: Total screen time composite (TV + video games + socializing) would fail Step 2, but the paper separately reports modality-specific analyses including a 'socializing' subtype (texting + video chatting + social media) which is exclusively phone/SM, rescuing IC2 via Step 1.
